summary: Observed Explorer runtime setting for Windows 11 taskbar alignment. Microsoft's Windows 11 settings reference documents the TaskbarAl setting family, the 25H2 raw registry dump lists the same value name under the current-user Explorer\Advanced branch, and a Procmon capture on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 shows Explorer reading HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl as REG_DWORD 0/1 during shell startup. The app writes that same runtime surface directly.

setting.name: Taskbar Alignment
setting.casual_explanation: Windows 11 can keep the taskbar icons centered or move them to the left. Some people prefer the older left-aligned layout.
setting.why_people_change_it: People move the taskbar left when they want a layout that feels closer to older Windows versions or want the Start button and pinned apps closer to the screen edge.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: This is a low-risk shell preference change. It affects taskbar layout only and does not disable a Windows feature.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft's Windows 11 settings reference describes the DesktopTaskbarAl setting family and its 0/1 left-versus-center semantics. The 25H2 raw registry dump lists TaskbarAl under the current-user Explorer\Advanced branch and under a siloed current-user Explorer\Advanced branch, which corroborates that the value family is still present on current builds. A Procmon capture on 2026-03-13 showed the live Explorer persistence surface on this host: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced -> TaskbarAl as REG_DWORD. During two Explorer restarts, reg.exe wrote TaskbarAl = 0 and TaskbarAl = 1, and Explorer.EXE immediately queried the same parent-key value both times. The documented TaskbarAl subkey with REG_SZ value name SystemSettings_DesktopTaskbar_Al was not observed in this runtime capture, so this record publishes the actual Explorer runtime surface that the app writes.

allowed_values.label: Left aligned
allowed_values.meaning: Taskbar icons and the Start button are aligned to the left. Procmon confirmed Explorer.EXE queries TaskbarAl = 0 after the value is written and Explorer restarts.

allowed_values.label: Center aligned
allowed_values.meaning: Taskbar icons are centered. Procmon confirmed Explorer.EXE queries TaskbarAl = 1 after the value is written and Explorer restarts.

targets.notes: The Windows 11 settings reference remains useful feature-level context, but this record validates the parent-key REG_DWORD surface because that is what Explorer consumed in the runtime capture.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes TaskbarAl = 0 under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced. Procmon confirmed Explorer.EXE consumes the same runtime surface on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037.

windows_defaults.label: Windows-managed alignment default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 11 systems with taskbar alignment support

states.rationale: This record validates the explicit runtime values 0 and 1. It does not publish a machine-checked missing-value default.

windows_defaults.notes: Windows 11 centers the taskbar by default at the UX level, but this record's machine-checked proof focuses on the explicit runtime storage values used by Explorer.

recommended_profiles.label: Left-aligned taskbar
intended_for: Users who prefer the classic left-aligned Windows layout
avoid_for: Users who prefer the Windows 11 centered layout

states.rationale: Procmon confirmed Explorer reads TaskbarAl = 0 as the left-aligned runtime state.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the taskbar layout to feel closer to older Windows releases.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want the default centered Windows 11 taskbar experience.
tradeoffs: Moves the Start button and taskbar icons to the left.
Does not disable any Windows feature.
Reversible by writing TaskbarAl = 1 and restarting Explorer.

recommended_profiles.label: Centered taskbar
intended_for: Users who want the standard Windows 11 layout

states.rationale: Procmon confirmed Explorer reads TaskbarAl = 1 as the centered runtime state.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the standard Windows 11 centered taskbar layout.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You prefer the classic left-aligned layout.
tradeoffs: Keeps the centered Windows 11 taskbar layout.
Does not disable any Windows feature.

evidence.title: Microsoft Windows 11 settings reference for taskbar alignment
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the taskbar alignment setting family and its 0/1 left-versus-center semantics, but this page does not match the parent-key REG_DWORD surface consumed by Explorer in the runtime capture.

evidence.title: 25H2 raw registry corroboration for TaskbarAl
evidence.summary: The 25H2 raw registry dump lists TaskbarAl under the current-user Explorer\Advanced branch and under a siloed current-user Explorer\Advanced branch, corroborating that the value family is still present on current builds.

evidence.title: Procmon capture - Explorer taskbar alignment runtime surface
evidence.summary: A reversible Procmon capture on 2026-03-13 wrote TaskbarAl = 0, restarted Explorer, restored TaskbarAl = 1, and restarted Explorer again. reg.exe wrote HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl as REG_DWORD 0 and 1, and Explorer.EXE queried the same value immediately after each restart. No runtime traffic for the documented TaskbarAl subkey with SystemSettings_DesktopTaskbar_Al was observed in this capture.

decision.why: Procmon on Windows 11 Pro 10.0.26200.8037 confirmed that Explorer consumes HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl as a REG_DWORD runtime setting with 0 = left and 1 = center. The 25H2 raw registry dump corroborates that the value family is still present on current builds. The app writes that same surface directly. The official settings reference remains useful feature-level context, but the runtime trace is the decisive proof for the persisted shell value.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: reg.exe RegSetValue HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl Type: REG_DWORD Data: 0; Explorer.EXE RegQueryValue HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl Type: REG_DWORD Data: 0; reg.exe RegSetValue HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl Type: REG_DWORD Data: 1; Explorer.EXE RegQueryValue HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarAl Type: REG_DWORD Data: 1
validation_proof.notes: The runtime capture proves the actual Explorer persistence surface used on this host. The documented TaskbarAl subkey with SystemSettings_DesktopTaskbar_Al was not observed in this trace.
